text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY CLINIC - SURVEILLANCE VISIT NOTE

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Margaret Osei is a 55-year-old postmenopausal woman with FIGO IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant) presenting for her 24-month post-chemotherapy surveillance visit. She is currently on niraparib maintenance therapy, now approximately 22 months into the maintenance phase.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Osei was diagnosed approximately two years ago with bilateral high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC (pT3c pN0 pM0), with peritoneal carcinomatosis and omental caking at presentation. CA-125 was markedly elevated at 1,847 U/mL at diagnosis. She underwent successful primary debulking surgery with complete gross resection (R0), and subsequently completed six cycles of adjuvant carboplatin (AUC 5) and paclitaxel, the latter dose-reduced to 135 mg/m2 from cycle 4 onward due to Grade 2 sensory peripheral neuropathy. Post-chemotherapy restaging CT demonstrated complete radiographic response, with CA-125 normalizing to 11 U/mL.

She was started on niraparib maintenance given her germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (BRCA1 c.5266dupC, pathogenic) and HRD-positive tumor. Initial dose was 200 mg daily, individualized based on weight and platelet parameters. She developed Grade 3 thrombocytopenia at week 6, requiring a 28-day hold and subsequent dose reduction to 100 mg daily. Since restarting at the reduced dose, she has done well with only Grade 1 thrombocytopenia (platelets ranging 128-143 x10^9/L on monthly monitoring) and has had no further significant hematologic toxicity.

Today she presents for her 24-month post-chemotherapy surveillance visit. She has been continuing niraparib 100 mg daily without interruption since her dose reduction, now approximately 22 months of total maintenance therapy. She reports feeling well overall. She has returned fully to her position as a high school biology teacher and describes her quality of life as very good. She endorses mild fatigue, primarily at the end of the school week, which she attributes to the demands of her work rather than the medication. She has mild intermittent nausea, Grade 0-1, no longer requiring scheduled antiemetics - she uses ondansetron only rarely. She denies any new abdominal pain, bloating, early satiety, change in bowel habits, new dyspnea, cough, or lower extremity edema. She denies any vaginal bleeding. She has no new neurologic symptoms.

She mentions that her daughter, now 27 years old, recently received results of BRCA1 genetic testing and was found to carry the familial BRCA1 pathogenic variant. The daughter has been referred to high-risk genetics and gynecology for counseling and surveillance planning. Ms. Osei finds this emotionally difficult but is glad her daughter knows and can be proactive.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive: mild intermittent fatigue (Grade 0-1), occasional nausea (Grade 0-1, rare). Mild hot flashes, managed. Vaginal dryness, ongoing, managed with topical estrogen.
Negative: no abdominal pain or distension, no new pelvic pressure, no change in urinary frequency or urgency, no hematuria, no hemoptysis, no unintentional weight loss, no fevers or night sweats, no arthralgias, no rash, no headaches, no visual changes. Peripheral neuropathy fully resolved (Grade 0).

PAST ONCOLOGIC HISTORY (summarized):
- High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma FIGO IIIC, bilateral; germline BRCA1 c.5266dupC (pathogenic); HRD-positive (score 54); TP53 p.R248W; MSS; TMB 5 mut/Mb
- Primary debulking surgery: TAH/BSO, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral pelvic peritoneal stripping, right hemidiaphragm peritonectomy; R0 resection achieved; pelvic and para-aortic nodes all negative (0/27)
- Adjuvant chemotherapy: Carboplatin AUC 5 / Paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 x3 cycles, then dose-reduced paclitaxel 135 mg/m2 x3 cycles (total 6 cycles) due to Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy
- Niraparib maintenance: started at 200 mg daily, reduced to 100 mg daily after Grade 3 thrombocytopenia; now ~22 months total on maintenance

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 74 bpm, RR 16, Temp 36.8 C, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 68 kg (stable).
General: Alert, well-appearing woman in no distress. Appears her stated age. Pleasant affect.
HEENT: No scleral icterus. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis.
Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No dullness to percussion.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No peripheral edema.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Midline laparotomy scar well-healed, without hernia or induration. No palpable masses. No hepatosplenomegaly. No shifting dullness. Bowel sounds present and normal.
Pelvic: Vaginal cuff intact, well-healed. No induration, no palpable nodularity in the vaginal cuff or parametria. No adnexal fullness appreciated on rectovaginal exam (post-surgical, limited exam).
Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. No lower extremity tenderness.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves intact. No focal deficits. Sensation grossly intact in bilateral lower extremities - patient confirms neuropathy fully resolved.

LABORATORY RESULTS:
CA-125: 10 U/mL (reference < 35 U/mL; baseline at diagnosis 1,847 U/mL) - stable, normalized
CBC:
  - WBC: 4.8 x10^9/L (reference 4.5-11.0)
  - Hemoglobin: 11.9 g/dL (reference 12.0-16.0) - mild, Grade 1 anemia, stable
  - Platelets: 138 x10^9/L (reference 150-400) - Grade 1 thrombocytopenia, stable; no further decline on current dose
CMP: BMP within normal limits. Creatinine 0.78 mg/dL, eGFR >60. LFTs normal. Electrolytes normal.
Note: Hgb mildly below normal (11.9) consistent with chronic mild anemia on PARP inhibitor, Grade 1 and stable over prior several months. No transfusion required.

IMAGING RESULTS:
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (24-month post-chemotherapy surveillance): No evidence of peritoneal carcinomatosis. No ascites. No new pelvic or abdominal masses. No lymphadenopathy. No hepatic lesions. Post-surgical changes stable. Impression: Sustained complete radiographic remission at 24-month surveillance. No evidence of disease recurrence. (See separate imaging report for full detail.)

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. HIGH-GRADE SEROUS OVARIAN CARCINOMA, FIGO IIIC - COMPLETE SUSTAINED REMISSION
Ms. Osei continues in complete remission at 24 months post-chemotherapy. CA-125 stable at 10 U/mL (normalized). CT imaging shows no evidence of disease recurrence. This is an excellent outcome. She has now been on niraparib maintenance for approximately 22 months. Per standard protocol aligned with the SOLO1 data (36-month maintenance duration), she is on track to complete maintenance therapy in approximately 14 more months. We will plan to continue niraparib through the 36-month mark barring toxicity or progression.

2. NIRAPARIB MAINTENANCE THERAPY - ONGOING
Continue niraparib 100 mg orally once daily at bedtime. Platelet counts have remained stable in the Grade 1 range (128-143 x10^9/L over the past several months) without further significant decline - no dose modification needed at this time. Mild Grade 1 anemia (Hgb 11.9) is stable and does not require intervention. Will continue monthly CBC monitoring. Patient is tolerating the medication well overall at this reduced dose.

3. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY - RESOLVED
Prior Grade 2 sensory peripheral neuropathy from paclitaxel has fully resolved. No active management required.

4. MENOPAUSAL SYMPTOMS
Patient continues on topical vaginal estrogen (local application) for vaginal dryness with good effect and venlafaxine for hot flash management. Continue current regimen. These remain appropriate in the post-surgical menopause setting for a patient with ovarian cancer on PARP inhibitor maintenance; systemic hormone therapy is not recommended.

5. FAMILY HISTORY / GENETIC IMPLICATIONS
Patient's daughter (age 27) was found to carry the familial BRCA1 pathogenic variant. Referred for high-risk surveillance and risk-reduction counseling through genetics and high-risk gynecology. The son's testing status unknown - patient encouraged to share results and offer cascade testing to other first-degree relatives.

6. COGNITIVE SYMPTOMS
Patient previously noted mild cognitive changes consistent with 'chemo brain' at her 12-month visit. She reports these are no longer significantly bothersome and she is functioning well professionally. No specific intervention at this time; reassurance provided.

SURVEILLANCE PLAN:
- CA-125 every 3 months
- CT abdomen/pelvis annually (next due in approximately 12 months unless symptoms arise)
- Clinical exam every 6 months
- Continue niraparib maintenance through 36-month mark (approximately 14 more months)
- Return to clinic in 3 months for CBC and CA-125 check

Patient verbalized understanding of the plan. She expressed gratitude for her ongoing remission and remains engaged in her care. All questions answered. Visit duration: 35 minutes.

Attending Gynecologic Oncologist
